chunk_summary: This section categorizes food hypersensitivity into immune-mediated (IgE, non-IgE, mixed) and nonimmune reactions, emphasizing diagnostic and therapeutic implications for elimination diets.
learning_objectives: Differentiate between immune-mediated and nonimmune food hypersensitivity reactions.
Understand the clinical presentation and mechanisms of IgE, non-IgE, and mixed immune food allergies.
Recognize nonimmune food reactions and their management without allergy testing.
Apply the taxonomy to prevent unnecessary elimination diets and associated risks.
key_facts: Food hypersensitivity includes both immune-mediated (food allergy) and nonimmune reactions.
IgE-mediated food allergy symptoms occur within minutes to 2 hours and include urticaria, wheeze, and cardiovascular compromise.
Non-IgE-mediated food allergy symptoms manifest hours to days later, often with gastrointestinal symptoms like diarrhea or blood in stool.
Nonimmune reactions include lactose intolerance, toxin-mediated reactions (e.g., scombroid), and pharmacologic effects (e.g., caffeine).
Broad elimination diets in nonimmune reactions can lead to iatrogenic undernutrition and caregiver anxiety.
Indian Academy of Pediatrics guidelines emphasize distinguishing immune food allergy from nonimmune intolerance.
reference_quote: In Indian guidance, the IAP food allergy standard treatment guideline distinguishes immune food allergy from nonimmune intolerance and frames food allergy as a reproducible immune response on exposure.
document_key_topics: Taxonomy of immune and nonimmune food reactions
Clinical implications of IgE and non-IgE food allergies
